articles.label: Public Records
articles.headline: Escambia County quietly settled three lawsuits last year. The terms remain sealed.
articles.dek: County commissioners approved the settlements on consent agendas with no discussion. Attorneys say the sealed terms raise questions about taxpayer liability and accountability.
brief: Escambia County settled three lawsuits in 2025, but the financial terms remain sealed from public view
All three settlements were approved on consent agendas without commissioner discussion or public comment
Legal experts say Florida law favors disclosure of settlement amounts involving public funds
articles.body:
The item appeared on page 14 of a 47-page agenda packet. Settlement of Case No. 2024-CA-002871. No dollar figure. No summary of the underlying claims. No staff recommendation beyond "approve."

Escambia County Commissioner Jeff Bergosh moved to approve the consent agenda. Commissioner Steven Barry seconded. The vote was 5-0. The entire process took less than eight seconds.

That scene repeated twice more during 2025 county commission meetings. Three lawsuits against Escambia County. Three settlements approved without a word of public discussion. Three sets of terms that remain sealed from the taxpayers who funded them.

The Flightline filed public records requests for the settlement agreements in February. The county attorney's office released redacted versions with the financial terms blacked out, citing ongoing confidentiality provisions negotiated as part of each settlement.

'The Public Has a Right to Know'

Florida has long been considered a national leader in government transparency. The state's public records law, Chapter 119, establishes a presumption that government documents are open to inspection. Exemptions exist but must be narrowly construed.

Settlement agreements involving public entities occupy contested ground.

"The general rule is that when you're spending taxpayer money, the public has a right to know how much and why," said Maria Santana, a Tallahassee-based attorney who specializes in media law. "Confidentiality clauses in government settlements are legally questionable. They're not automatically enforceable just because both parties agreed to them."

The three Escambia County settlements involved an employment discrimination claim filed by a former Public Works employee, a personal injury lawsuit stemming from a vehicle accident involving a county work truck, and a contract dispute with a vendor whose name was also redacted.

County Attorney Alison Rogers declined to discuss specifics but defended the process in a written statement.

"Each settlement was negotiated in good faith to protect the county's interests and minimize litigation costs," Rogers wrote. "Confidentiality provisions are sometimes necessary to reach resolution. The Board of County Commissioners reviewed and approved each agreement in accordance with Florida law."

Consent Agenda, No Discussion

Consent agendas are standard practice in local government. Routine matters—contract renewals, budget transfers, meeting minutes—get bundled together for a single vote. The practice saves time. It also means items can pass without scrutiny unless a commissioner specifically pulls one for discussion.

None of the three settlements were pulled.

Commissioner Barry, reached by phone, said he reviewed each settlement before voting.

"I trust our county attorney to negotiate in the county's best interest," Barry said. "These are legal matters. Sometimes discretion is appropriate."

Asked whether taxpayers deserve to know how much the county paid to resolve the lawsuits, Barry paused.

"That's a fair question," he said. "I'd have to think about that."

Commissioner Bergosh did not respond to multiple requests for comment. Neither did commissioners May, Underhill or Robinson.

A Pattern Across Florida

Escambia County is not unique. A 2024 review by the First Amendment Foundation found that at least 23 Florida counties approved lawsuit settlements with confidential terms over a three-year period. The total dollar amount remains unknown.

"It's become standard practice, and that's the problem," said Bobby Block, executive director of the foundation. "Government attorneys will tell you confidentiality is necessary to get cases settled. But necessary for whom? The plaintiff who wants privacy? Or the government that wants to avoid embarrassment?"

Some Florida jurisdictions have moved toward greater transparency. Miami-Dade County adopted a policy in 2023 requiring disclosure of settlement amounts exceeding $50,000. Pinellas County releases all settlement terms unless a court specifically orders them sealed.

Escambia County has no such policy.

What the Records Show

The redacted settlement agreements obtained by The Flightline reveal limited information.

The employment discrimination case, filed in 2024, alleged a hostile work environment and retaliation. The plaintiff, a 12-year county employee, claimed she was passed over for promotions after filing an internal complaint. The settlement includes a non-disparagement clause and a provision prohibiting the plaintiff from seeking future employment with the county.

The vehicle accident case involved a county dump truck that struck a passenger vehicle on Mobile Highway in 2023. The plaintiff sustained injuries described in court filings as "severe and permanent." The county's insurance carrier was involved in the settlement negotiations, according to the redacted agreement.

The contract dispute case is the most opaque. The vendor's name, the nature of the contract and the substance of the dispute were all redacted. The agreement runs 11 pages. Eight of them are almost entirely blacked out.

Legal Questions Remain

Whether the county can legally withhold settlement amounts is unclear. Florida courts have issued mixed rulings on confidentiality provisions in government settlements.

A 1995 Attorney General opinion concluded that settlement amounts paid by public entities are public records and cannot be made confidential by agreement. But some courts have allowed confidentiality when disclosure would harm ongoing litigation or when the opposing party demanded secrecy as a condition of settlement.

"The law is murky, and government attorneys exploit that," Santana said. "If a newspaper or citizen wanted to challenge these redactions, they'd have to go to court. That costs money and takes time. Most people don't have either."

The Flightline has requested an opinion from the county attorney's office on the legal basis for the redactions. Rogers acknowledged the request but said a formal response would take "several weeks."

No Oversight, No Accountability

The three settlements raise a broader question about county governance: Who is watching?

Escambia County has no inspector general, no independent auditor and no standing committee that reviews legal settlements. The county attorney reports directly to the commission but negotiates settlements largely without public input.

"You've got a system where the lawyers handle everything behind closed doors, and the commissioners rubber-stamp it," said Tom Garner, a Pensacola attorney and former county commission candidate. "That's not how self-government is supposed to work."

Garner said he has no inside knowledge of the three settlements but finds the lack of transparency troubling.

"If these were good settlements that saved taxpayer money, why not say so? The secrecy suggests there's something they don't want people to see."

The next county commission meeting is scheduled for May 1. The agenda has not yet been posted.

articles.label: Development
articles.headline: The Palafox parking problem nobody wants to solve
articles.dek: Downtown business owners say weekend crowds are great for sales but murder on access. The city's 2019 parking study recommended a garage. Seven years later, the lot behind Seville Quarter is still gravel.
brief: A 2019 city-commissioned parking study recommended building a downtown garage within five years
Business owners report customers leaving after circling blocks and finding no spots on weekend nights
The gravel lot behind Seville Quarter remains the only significant parking expansion since 2017
articles.body:
Saturday night on Palafox Street. The sidewalks are packed. Every restaurant has a wait. The rooftop bars are three deep. And somewhere on Garden Street, a couple from Mobile is making their fourth lap around the block, watching the same group of teenagers cross the same intersection, wondering if they should just go home.

They're not alone. Ask any downtown business owner about their best nights and their worst problem, and you'll hear the same answer twice: the crowds.

"We had a woman call us crying last month," said Elena Marchetti, general manager at a Palafox Street restaurant she asked not to name for fear of discouraging customers. "She'd driven down from Brewton for her anniversary. Spent forty minutes looking for parking. By the time she found a spot six blocks away, her reservation was gone. We couldn't hold it. We were slammed."

Marchetti said she's heard some version of this story dozens of times since 2023. The downtown renaissance everyone wanted has arrived. The infrastructure to support it has not.

The study that went nowhere

In 2019, the City of Pensacola paid $87,000 for a comprehensive downtown parking study. The consultant, Walker Consultants, delivered a 94-page report that December. Its primary recommendation: build a parking structure within five years to accommodate projected growth.

The report identified three potential sites. It projected that by 2024, weekend demand would exceed supply by roughly 400 spaces. It recommended the city begin design work immediately.

Seven years later, no garage exists. No design work has been completed. No site has been selected.

"The study was good work," said David Hernandez, who served on the city council from 2018 to 2022 and voted to commission it. "And then COVID hit, and everything stopped. But COVID's been over for years now. I don't know what the holdup is."

The current city administration declined multiple interview requests for this story. A spokesperson provided a written statement: "The city continues to evaluate parking solutions for downtown Pensacola and is committed to supporting our business community."

The statement did not address the 2019 study or explain why its recommendations were not implemented.

The gravel solution

The most visible parking expansion in recent years sits behind Seville Quarter: a gravel lot that holds roughly 120 vehicles. It opened in 2017 as a temporary measure.

It's still there. Still gravel. Still temporary.

On a recent Friday night, every space was full by 7:30 p.m. A hand-lettered sign near the entrance read "LOT FULL" in red marker. An attendant in a reflective vest waved cars away while pointing vaguely toward Government Street.

"I tell them try the courthouse lot," the attendant said. He declined to give his name. "But that fills up too. After that, I don't know what to tell them."

The courthouse lot, owned by Escambia County, allows public parking on weekends. It adds roughly 200 spaces to downtown's supply. On any night when Vinyl Music Hall has a show, or when Blue Wahoos games let out, or when the weather is good enough to draw the usual Palafox crowds, those 200 spaces disappear within an hour of sunset.

What business owners want

The Downtown Improvement Board, a special taxing district funded by downtown property owners, has been pushing for parking solutions since at least 2018. Its current executive director, Marcus Webb, said the board has presented three different proposals to the city council over the past four years.

"Each time, it gets studied, discussed, and tabled," Webb said. "Nobody wants to be the person who votes to spend $15 million on a parking garage. It's not sexy. It doesn't cut a ribbon well. But it's what we need."

Webb said the DIB's most recent proposal, presented in September 2025, called for a public-private partnership that would reduce the city's upfront cost. A developer had expressed interest in building a mixed-use structure with ground-floor retail, apartments above, and 350 public parking spaces. The city's contribution would have been the land—the same gravel lot behind Seville Quarter that it already owns.

The proposal was referred to the city's planning department for review. No timeline was given. Webb said he hasn't received an update since.

"We're trying to make this as easy as possible," he said. "At some point, you have to wonder if anyone actually wants to solve this."

The cost of inaction

Measuring the economic impact of parking shortages is difficult. No one tracks how many visitors give up and go home. No one surveys the couple from Mobile who ended up eating at a chain restaurant near the interstate instead.

But business owners say they see it in their numbers.

"Our Friday and Saturday revenue should be higher than it is," said Thomas Chen, owner of a bar on South Palafox. "We've got the demand. The foot traffic is there—if you can get here. We're leaving money on the table because people can't park."

Chen said he's considered validating parking at a private garage on Baylen Street, but the cost—$8 per vehicle—would eat into margins that are already thin.

"I can't afford to subsidize the city's failure to plan," he said.

Meanwhile, the same dynamics that make parking scarce are making it more valuable. Surface lots that once charged $5 on weekends now charge $15. The gravel lot behind Seville Quarter is $10. For a family of four driving downtown for dinner, parking can add $20 to the cost of the evening before anyone orders a drink.

What happens next

The city council's next regular meeting is May 4. Parking is not on the agenda.

Webb said the DIB plans to request a formal presentation slot at the June meeting to revisit the mixed-use proposal. He's not optimistic.

"We've been here before," he said. "We'll make our case. They'll nod along. And then six months from now, I'll be standing in front of them again, making the same case, hoping something's different."

Back on Garden Street, the couple from Mobile has given up. They're heading north on Palafox, toward the interstate, toward whatever's open near their hotel. Downtown Pensacola glows in their rearview mirror.

"It looked really nice," the woman says. "Maybe next time."

Maybe. If they can find a spot.

articles.label: Military
articles.headline: Blue Angels homecoming week brings 200,000 visitors. It also brings friction.
articles.dek: Pensacola Beach residents brace for the July airshow's return with a mix of civic pride and noise complaints. Short-term rental bookings are already full. So are the code enforcement dockets.
brief: The July 2026 Blue Angels Homecoming Airshow is projected to draw 200,000 visitors to Pensacola Beach over three days, with short-term rental occupancy already at 98% capacity
Santa Rosa County code enforcement has logged 47 noise and parking complaints tied to airshow week in the past two years, prompting new rental registration requirements
Escambia County tourism officials estimate a $28 million economic impact, while some year-round residents push for flight path adjustments over residential areas
articles.body:
The first pass comes low over Via de Luna Drive around 11 a.m. Six F/A-18 Super Hornets in tight formation, close enough that you feel the sound before you hear it. Windows rattle in the condos along Portofino. Car alarms trip in the Margaritaville parking lot. A toddler on the beach starts crying. His grandmother starts cheering.

This is what homecoming sounds like in Pensacola Beach.

The Blue Angels Homecoming Airshow returns July 10-12 to Naval Air Station Pensacola, the squadron's winter training base and spiritual home since 1949. Event organizers project 200,000 attendees across the three-day event. Short-term rental platforms show occupancy on the beach already at 98% for that week. Hotels in Gulf Breeze and downtown Pensacola are following close behind.

The economic math is straightforward. Visit Pensacola, the county's tourism marketing arm, estimates a $28 million impact from airshow weekend—hotel taxes, restaurant receipts, rental car fees, the whole apparatus of visitors spending money in concentrated bursts.

"This is our Super Bowl," said Darien Schaefer, president of Visit Pensacola. "There's no other single event that drives this level of visitation and national attention to our destination."

The attention, though, cuts more than one way.

The complaint docket

Santa Rosa County's code enforcement office has logged 47 formal complaints tied to Blue Angels week over the past two years. The grievances cluster around the same issues: illegal parking on residential streets, overcrowded rentals exceeding occupancy limits, noise from parties that start when the jets stop flying and continue past midnight.

Portside Villas, a condominium complex a quarter-mile from the Pensacola Beach Boardwalk, submitted a petition to Santa Rosa County commissioners in March requesting enhanced patrol presence during airshow week. The petition cited "ongoing quality of life concerns" and was signed by 34 unit owners.

"We're not against the airshow," said Margaret Delcambre, the HOA president who organized the petition. "We're against being overwhelmed by people who treat our neighborhood like a parking lot and a party venue. There's a difference."

The county responded in part. New short-term rental registration requirements, approved by commissioners in February, mandate that all rental properties on Pensacola Beach display registration numbers in their listings and provide 24-hour contact information for a local property manager. Fines for violations start at $500.

Whether enforcement will match the new rules remains an open question. Santa Rosa County employs three code enforcement officers for the entire beach area.

Flight paths and frustration

The friction extends beyond rental regulations. A subset of year-round Pensacola Beach residents—a population of roughly 4,500 people who live on the barrier island full-time—have raised concerns about the airshow's practice flight schedule.

The Blue Angels conduct practice sessions over Pensacola Beach in the weeks leading up to the show, typically between 11:30 a.m. and noon on Tuesdays and Wednesdays. The flight demonstrations last about 45 minutes. They are loud. Extremely loud.

Decibel readings taken by a resident on Ariola Drive during a 2025 practice session registered 127 dB during a low pass—equivalent to standing near a jackhammer or a military jet at takeoff. The threshold for potential hearing damage is 85 dB.

A petition circulated last fall requesting that the Navy adjust practice flight paths to route over open water rather than directly over residential areas collected 89 signatures. The Navy responded through a public affairs officer that flight paths are determined by safety requirements and cannot be altered to accommodate noise concerns.

"The Blue Angels flight demonstration profile is designed to showcase the precision and capabilities of naval aviation for spectators in designated viewing areas," the statement read. "The demonstration area and flight paths are established in coordination with the FAA and cannot be modified without compromising the safety and integrity of the performance."

Some residents found the response unsatisfying.

"They're saying the quiet part out loud," said Don Kirkpatrick, a retired airline pilot who has lived on Pensacola Beach since 2019. "The show is designed for spectators. We just happen to live underneath it."

The pride calculation

Not everyone on the beach shares the frustration. For many residents, the Blue Angels represent something larger than the sum of their noise complaints—a connection to Pensacola's military identity and a source of genuine civic pride.

The squadron has called Pensacola home for more than 75 years. The National Naval Aviation Museum, located on NAS Pensacola, draws nearly a million visitors annually. Blue Angels merchandise fills gift shops from the beach to downtown. The squadron's aircraft are painted on water towers, murals, and the sides of buildings throughout the region.

"You can't separate Pensacola from the Blue Angels," said Frank Martinez, owner of Paradise Bar & Grill on Pensacola Beach. "People who move here and then complain about the jets—I don't know what to tell them. Did you not see the giant airplane on the welcome sign?"

Martinez said airshow week accounts for roughly 15% of his annual revenue. He hires extra staff, extends hours, and pre-orders twice his normal inventory of beer and oysters.

"Three days of chaos, yeah," he said. "But three days that keep us going through the slow months."

What's new in 2026

This year's homecoming includes several changes from previous events. The Navy has expanded the designated spectator area on NAS Pensacola by approximately 12 acres, adding capacity for an estimated 15,000 additional attendees on base. A new shuttle system will run from parking areas at Pensacola International Airport, an attempt to reduce congestion on Navy Boulevard and Barrancas Avenue.

Off-base viewing areas on Pensacola Beach will see increased presence from Santa Rosa County Sheriff's deputies, according to Sheriff Bob Johnson. The county approved overtime funding for 18 additional deputies during the three-day event.

"We want people to enjoy the show," Johnson said at a March press conference. "We also want residents to be able to get in and out of their neighborhoods. It's a balance."

The balance, as always, will be tested.

Short-term rental bookings suggest the beach will absorb somewhere between 8,000 and 12,000 visitors on its busiest nights—a population surge of roughly 200% above normal for the four-mile stretch of sand and condominiums. Traffic on the Bob Sikes Bridge, the only vehicle access point to Pensacola Beach, regularly backs up for an hour or more during airshow afternoons.

The toddler on the beach will grow up, eventually, and maybe he'll cheer too. Or maybe he'll move somewhere quieter. The jets will keep coming either way.

Homecoming is in July. Pensacola Beach is already bracing.
